summary: Gruber recommends Quinn, a Tetris clone for OS X discovered by Brent Simmons. He praises its faithful gameplay, smooth animation, and configurable controls. He highlights the developer's attention to detail, particularly the decision to remove the Help menu entirely rather than leave a useless placeholder. The post is a brief but enthusiastic endorsement of a well-crafted freeware Mac game.
key_points: Quinn is a faithful Tetris clone for OS X with excellent gameplay, smooth animation, and configurable keystrokes
The game includes an Undo feature that compensates for accidental mis-drops, a practical if technically cheating concession
The developer removed the Help menu entirely rather than leaving a vestigial menu item with a useless dialog — a detail Gruber finds admirable
Good software design means knowing what to leave out, including unnecessary UI elements
money_quotes: Quinn is faithful to the original game — as Brent writes: no dumb "innovations" or anything. Right on.

Mr. Härtel had the common decency to remove the Help menu entirely, rather than leave behind a single vestigial menu item that brings up an idiotic "Help is not implemented for this application" dialog box.
It's just a great little Mac app.
key_insight: Good software craftsmanship shows in the small details, like removing a Help menu rather than leaving behind a useless placeholder.
